text.title: API Authentication Methods
text.summary: API keys, JWT, and OAuth 2.0 flows — when to use each, implementation patterns, and scope management.

rule.statement: Choose the simplest mechanism that fits the trust boundary: API keys for server-to-server, JWT for user sessions, OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code + PKCE for third-party delegation, mTLS for zero-trust machine-to-machine.
rule.rationale: Over-engineering auth increases attack surface. OAuth is overkill for server-to-server; API keys are insufficient for user sessions with revocation requirements.

rule.statement: Store API keys hashed (HMAC-SHA-256 or argon2). Use a human-readable prefix (sk_live_, sk_test_) for identification. Never display after initial issuance.
rule.rationale: API keys stored in plaintext are unrecoverable secrets on breach. The prefix allows identifying which key was used without exposing the full value.

example.code:
import hashlib from fastapi import Header, HTTPException
async def verify_api_key(x_api_key: str = Header(...)): hashed = hashlib.sha256(x_api_key.encode()).hexdigest() key = await db.api_keys.find_one(hashed=hashed) if not key or key.revoked: raise HTTPException(401, "Invalid API key") return key

rule.statement: Access tokens expire in 5–15 minutes. Refresh tokens rotate on every use; on reuse detection, invalidate the entire token family and force re-authentication.
rule.rationale: Short-lived access tokens limit breach window. Refresh token rotation detects theft: if a stolen token is used, the legitimate holder's rotation attempt reuses the old token, triggering family invalidation.

rule.statement: Validate iss, aud, exp, nbf, and kid on every token. Reject alg:none and any algorithm not in an explicit allowlist. Use RS256/EdDSA when verifiers are distributed.
rule.rationale: Missing aud/iss checks allow tokens from other services to be replayed. alg:none is a trivially-exploitable bypass. HS256 with shared secret is unsafe when multiple services hold the key.

rule.statement: Use Authorization Code + PKCE for all browser and mobile clients. Never implement Implicit Grant (deprecated) or Password Grant (deprecated) for new integrations.
rule.rationale: Implicit grant exposes tokens in URL fragments visible to third-party scripts. Password grant exposes user credentials to the client application. Both are deprecated in OAuth 2.1.

rule.statement: Scope names follow the pattern capability:resource (read:users, write:billing). Never grant admin as a casual delegation scope.
rule.rationale: Over-broad scopes defeat the purpose of OAuth delegation. Granular scopes allow users to grant minimal necessary access.

text.reference: FastAPI JWT create/verify with full claims validation
